text: All right, this is an important case. This is a twenty-five-year-old patient with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma who had severe lymphadenopathy.
The surgical field reveals a distended abdomen with visible pericholecystic fluid and significant edema surrounding the gallbladder.
Wasn't experiencing significant abdominal pain, but I think he's just been through so much that he just has a high pain threshold.

You can see him choking up in this gallbladder down by the infundibulum because there's just literally no working space in the abdomen. He has significant lymphadenopathy. He has hepatic disease, splenic disease, and he has bony mets.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to guide the dissection of the gallbladder, which is positioned relatively intrahepatic at the infundibulum. There is a visible presence of pericholecystic fluid surrounding the area.
The surgeon identifies the duct positioned deep within the surgical field and attempts to maneuver the artery out of the way to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon uses a round tip scissor to dissect the lateral portion of the gallbladder, maneuvering carefully in the limited working space.
The surgeon observes the presence of pericholecystic fluid and notes that the cystic plate appears completely edematous, indicating swelling in the tissue surrounding the gallbladder.

The surgeon attempts to obtain the critical view of safety, revealing the back surface of the liver. The tissue layers are still too thin, indicating ongoing dissection to achieve the necessary exposure.
The surgeon uses gentle cautery to free the cystic plate, which appears edematous and highly vascular, with visible pericholecystic fluid surrounding the area.
